project_and_agency_information.lead_agency: City of Riverbend Community Development Department

project_location.cross_streets: W. Oak Avenue and Riverbend Parkway
project_location.within_2_miles_schools: Riverbend High School; Oak Ridge Elementary School; Sierra Valley Charter Academy

development_type.development_educational: On-site early childhood education center (approximately 6,000 sq. ft.) intended to operate as a private preschool/daycare facility with capacity for 72 children, including two toddler classrooms, two preschool classrooms, indoor multipurpose space, administrative offices, and an outdoor secured play yard of approximately 8,500 sq. ft.
development_type.development_recreational: Approximately 6.3 acres of recreational amenities, including: (1) a central 3.2-acre neighborhood park with a multi-use turf field, picnic areas, shade structures, playground equipment for both younger and older children, a half-court basketball area, and walking paths; (2) a 1.1-acre greenway with fitness stations and community garden plots; (3) two smaller pocket parks totaling 0.7 acres with seating, shade trees, and informal play lawns; and (4) an internal trail system (about 1.3 acres) connecting to the regional riverfront trail along the American River.
development_type.development_water_facilities_type: Internal potable water distribution lines, two new 8-inch water mains connecting to existing city infrastructure in W. Oak Avenue, one pressure-reducing valve station, and upgraded fire hydrant network; no new off-site treatment plant proposed.
development_type.development_transportation_type: Construction of a new public collector roadway (Riverbend Parkway extension) with landscaped medians, on-street parking, Class II bike lanes, and sidewalks; installation of a new signalized intersection at W. Oak Avenue and Riverbend Parkway; internal local streets and alleys serving residential units; improvements to two existing bus stops on W. Oak Avenue including shelters, benches, and ADA-compliant pads; and construction of approximately 0.9 miles of multi-use trail connections to the regional trail system.
development_type.development_power_type: Rooftop solar photovoltaic systems on residential and commercial buildings (estimated aggregate capacity of approximately 1.8 MW), along with conventional grid service from the local electric utility; no standalone power plant proposed.
development_type.development_waste_treatment_type: Connection to the City of Riverbend sanitary sewer system via new 10-inch gravity sewer mains and a short off-site extension to the existing trunk line in Riverbend Parkway; no new wastewater treatment plant proposed.
development_type.development_hazardous_waste_type: No dedicated hazardous waste facility. Project will generate typical household and commercial hazardous wastes (e.g., cleaning supplies, paints, automotive fluids) that will be managed through existing citywide household hazardous waste collection programs and licensed commercial waste haulers.
development_type.development_other: A 12,000 sq. ft. community center with flexible meeting rooms, a small demonstration kitchen, co-working space, and a shared maker/hobby room; and an approximately 1,500 sq. ft. neighborhood-serving health clinic space to be leased to a regional nonprofit healthcare provider.

project_issues_discussed_in_document.project_issues_other: Energy consumption and greenhouse gas emissions; environmental justice considerations, including distribution of project benefits and potential burdens on sensitive populations in nearby neighborhoods.

present_land_use_and_project_description.present_land_use_zoning_gp_designation: The approximately 48.6-acre project site is currently designated in the City of Riverbend General Plan as "Business Park" (BP) with an overlay for "Mixed Use Opportunity Area" along the W. Oak Avenue frontage. Existing zoning is primarily BP (Business Park) with a small portion zoned R-1 (Single-Family Residential) along the southern boundary where an older subdivision was partially constructed in the 1970s but never completed. The site is largely vacant and disturbed grassland, with scattered non-native trees, remnant foundations from previous light industrial uses, and one abandoned metal storage building of approximately 4,500 sq. ft. There are no active agricultural operations on the site, and the surrounding land uses include single-family neighborhoods to the south and east, a small commercial strip center and gas station at the northwest corner of W. Oak Avenue and Riverbend Parkway, and a community park and high school to the west across Riverbend Parkway. The site is within the City limits and the City of Riverbend Urban Services Boundary, and is served by existing municipal water, sewer, and storm drainage infrastructure in adjacent roadways.
present_land_use_and_project_description.project_description:
The Riverbend Mixed-Use Village Project is a comprehensive infill development proposal intended to transform an underutilized, mostly vacant 48.6-acre parcel into a walkable, transit-supportive neighborhood that integrates housing, neighborhood-serving commercial uses, employment opportunities, and public amenities. The project reflects the City of Riverbend’s adopted vision for a compact, mixed-use corridor along W. Oak Avenue and responds to local and regional housing needs, especially for workforce and moderate-income households.

Key components of the project include:

1. Residential Development (262 total units)
- Approximately 118 single-family attached townhomes organized in clusters of 4 to 8 units, with a mix of two- and three-story designs. Typical units range from 1,350 to 1,850 square feet, with small private patios or balconies and one- or two-car garages accessed via rear alleys.
- Approximately 96 multi-family apartment units in three-story buildings located closer to W. Oak Avenue and the central park, with a mix of one- and two-bedroom units (approximately 650–1,050 square feet). These units are intended to be generally more affordable and to provide opportunities for young professionals, small families, and seniors.
- Approximately 48 senior-oriented flats in two elevator-served buildings, with universal design features such as wider hallways, roll-in showers, and enhanced accessibility. These buildings will be located adjacent to the community center and neighborhood health clinic to support aging-in-place.
- Residential densities range from about 8 to 28 dwelling units per acre across different subareas, consistent with the City’s Mixed-Use Village overlay standards. Overall residential density across the residential portions of the site is approximately 15 units per net acre.

2. Neighborhood Commercial and Office Uses
- Approximately 48,000 square feet of ground-floor neighborhood-serving commercial space along W. Oak Avenue and at the primary entrance from Riverbend Parkway. Anticipated uses include a small-format grocery or specialty food market (about 18,000 square feet), cafés and restaurants, personal services (e.g., hair salon, dry cleaner drop-off), and small professional offices.
- Approximately 35,000 square feet of two- to three-story office space oriented around a small plaza near the W. Oak Avenue/Riverbend Parkway intersection. These offices are intended to accommodate medical and dental practices, financial services, real estate and insurance offices, and other professional services that support both local residents and the broader community. Office buildings are designed with ground-floor transparency and active frontages to create a pedestrian-friendly streetscape.

3. Community Facilities and Recreation
- A 3.2-acre centrally located neighborhood park designed as the focal point of the project. The park will include a multi-use turf field suitable for informal soccer and youth sports, a children’s play area with age-separated equipment, picnic tables and barbecues, shade trees, walking paths, and seating areas. The park will be publicly accessible and dedicated to the City.
- A 12,000-square-foot community center building adjacent to the park, which will contain flexible meeting rooms that can be reserved by homeowners’ associations, youth groups, and local nonprofits; a small demonstration kitchen for cooking classes and events; co-working and study space with high-speed internet; and a shared maker/hobby room with workbenches and storage for arts, crafts, and DIY projects. The community center is envisioned as a hub for neighborhood activities, such as after-school programs, senior exercise classes, and cultural events.
- An approximately 6,000-square-foot early childhood education center (private preschool/daycare) with capacity for about 72 children, located on the eastern side of the site near existing residential neighborhoods to facilitate walk-in enrollment. The facility will include indoor classrooms, a multipurpose room, administrative offices, and a secure outdoor play yard with age-appropriate equipment and shade structures.
- A network of greenways, pocket parks, and multi-use trails totaling about 6.3 acres of recreational and open space. This includes a 1.1-acre linear greenway with fitness stations and seating, two smaller neighborhood pocket parks, and about 0.9 miles of multi-use paths that connect to the existing regional trail along the American River.

4. Circulation, Access, and Parking
- Extension and improvement of Riverbend Parkway through the project site, including a landscaped median, on-street parking, Class II bike lanes, street trees, and enhanced pedestrian crossings. The project will also construct a new signalized intersection at W. Oak Avenue and Riverbend Parkway, coordinated with the City’s traffic signal timing.
- Internal local residential streets and alleyways designed with traffic-calming features, including narrow travel lanes, bulb-outs at intersections, and frequent crosswalks to encourage low vehicle speeds and pedestrian safety.
- Sidewalks on both sides of all public streets, with direct pedestrian connections to bus stops on W. Oak Avenue. The project will upgrade two existing bus stops with shelters, benches, lighting, and ADA-compliant access pads.
- A comprehensive parking strategy including a mix of private garages for townhomes, surface parking lots for commercial and community uses, and on-street parking. Parking supply will meet or modestly exceed City minimum standards, with shared parking arrangements between the office, commercial, and community center uses to reduce the overall footprint of paved parking areas.

5. Infrastructure and Utilities
- Connection to existing City of Riverbend water, sewer, and storm drainage systems. The project includes construction of new on-site water distribution mains, fire hydrants, and a pressure-reducing valve station, as well as a new 10-inch gravity sewer main that ties into the existing trunk sewer in Riverbend Parkway.
- A stormwater management system incorporating low-impact development (LID) features such as bioretention planters, landscaped swales, and permeable paving in selected parking areas. Runoff will be treated and detained on-site before being discharged into the City’s storm drain system, which ultimately drains to Willow Creek and the American River.
- Undergrounding of new electrical and telecommunications utilities within the project site, along with installation of energy-efficient LED street lighting and provisions for electric vehicle (EV) charging infrastructure in commercial parking areas and some residential garages.

6. Sustainability and Design Features
- All residential and commercial buildings will be designed to meet or exceed current California Building Energy Efficiency Standards (Title 24), with high-performance building envelopes, efficient HVAC systems, and low-flow water fixtures.
- The project proposes rooftop solar photovoltaic installations on most residential buildings and on the flat roofs of commercial and office structures, with an estimated aggregate capacity of approximately 1.8 MW. This is intended to offset a substantial portion of the project’s electrical demand and support the City’s greenhouse gas reduction goals.
- The site plan emphasizes walkability and bicycle connectivity, with short block lengths, abundant shade trees, and direct access to the regional multi-use trail along the American River. Bicycle parking is provided at all commercial buildings and the community center, and internal streets are designed to accommodate safe cycling.

7. Phasing and Construction
- The project is anticipated to be constructed in three primary phases over approximately 6 to 8 years, subject to market conditions. Phase 1 will likely include initial infrastructure (roads and utilities), the central park, the community center shell building, and a portion of the residential units. Phase 2 will focus on the remaining residential components and the early childhood education center. Phase 3 will complete the office and commercial buildings along W. Oak Avenue and Riverbend Parkway.
- Construction activities will generally occur between 7:00 a.m. and 7:00 p.m. on weekdays, consistent with the City’s noise ordinance. The Mitigated Negative Declaration identifies best management practices (BMPs) for dust control, noise attenuation, traffic management, and protection of biological and cultural resources during grading and building construction.

Overall, the Riverbend Mixed-Use Village Project is intended to create a cohesive, pedestrian-oriented neighborhood that provides a variety of housing types, supports local businesses and services, and leverages existing infrastructure and transit along W. Oak Avenue. The associated Mitigated Negative Declaration evaluates potential environmental impacts in detail and identifies project design features and mitigation measures to reduce impacts to a less-than-significant level where feasible.
